explicit_text: I was part of Southern Assistance Volunteer Efforts, or SAVE, a club we formed in New York to support the Movement. We mainly raised funds to cover jail fees and other needs for activists. During the St. Augustine protests in 1964, SAVE helped by fundraising along the Eastern Seaboard, including Massachusetts. It was important work because it helped sustain the Movement financially and morally.
implicit_text: I was part of a volunteer club we formed in New York to support the Movement. We mainly raised funds to cover jail fees and other needs for activists. explicit_text: I was raised in New York City and was active in civil rights efforts there before and after my involvement in St. Augustine. I helped keep schools open during the teachers' strike by bringing in substitute teachers. After my arrest in St. Augustine, I faced discrimination in New York, including trouble getting into nursing school and losing jobs due to my arrest record. Eventually, I worked for 30 years at Isabella Geriatric Center in New York City and retired in 2000.
implicit_text: I was raised in the largest city in New York State and was active in civil rights efforts there before and after my involvement in St. Augustine. I helped keep schools open during the teachers' strike by bringing in substitute teachers. After my arrest in St. Augustine, I faced discrimination in that city, including trouble getting into nursing school and losing jobs due to my arrest record. Eventually, I worked for 30 years at a geriatric center in that city and retired in 2000.

explicit_text: I was born in St. Augustine but raised in New York. In 1964, I returned to St. Augustine to participate in the civil rights Movement, joining sit-ins and protests. I was arrested there after sitting at the lunch counter in St. George Pharmacy, and my time in jail helped energize the local community. Although I faced hostility and fear among locals, my involvement helped push the Movement forward in that city.

I was arrested there after sitting at the lunch counter in a local pharmacy, and my time in jail helped energize the local community. Although I faced hostility and fear among locals, my involvement helped push the Movement forward in that city.

explicit_text: I had been to Riverside Church in New York where Reverend William Sloane Coffin was a leader. It was there that plans were made for Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. to come to St. Augustine for Easter Sunday in 1964. The church was a hub for organizing and supporting civil rights activities, and I was connected to it through my activism.

He was part of the leadership supporting Dr. King’s planned visit to St. Augustine in 1964. His presence at Riverside Church was significant in organizing and encouraging activists like myself to participate in the civil rights efforts.

explicit_text: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. was supposed to come to St. Augustine for Easter Sunday in 1964, but when I arrived, he was still in Tallahassee and came later. His leadership was central to the Movement, and I had worked with people connected to him, like Dr. Hayling. The events in St. Augustine during that time were part of the larger struggle Dr. King was leading across the South.

explicit_text: The group I was with traveled to St. Augustine by Greyhound bus in 1964 to join the civil rights protests. It was a common way for activists from New York and other places to get down South to participate. The journey marked the beginning of a significant weekend of demonstrations and arrests.

explicit_text: During the 1964 St. Augustine protests, the Governor of Massachusetts' mother, Mrs. Peabody, was expected to come down to support the Movement. Her presence was part of the heightened attention and activity during that important weekend. It showed how the Movement attracted allies from outside the South.

explicit_text: Endicott Peabody was the Governor of Massachusetts during the 1964 St. Augustine civil rights protests. His mother, Mrs. Peabody, was supposed to come to St. Augustine that weekend to show support. This connection highlighted the national attention the Movement was gaining at the time.

explicit_text: I was arrested at the St. George Pharmacy in St. Augustine during a sit-in at the lunch counter in 1964. I went there with another man to participate in the protest against segregation. The police came with dogs and arrested us, which was a frightening but pivotal moment in my involvement with the Movement.
implicit_text: I was arrested at a local pharmacy in St. Augustine during a sit-in at the lunch counter in 1964. I went there with another man to participate in the protest against segregation. The police came with dogs and arrested us, which was a frightening but pivotal moment in my involvement with the Movement.

explicit_text: Elks Rest was the lodge on Washington Street in St. Augustine where we had our Movement headquarters during the 1964 protests. It was from there that groups organized to go uptown for sit-ins and demonstrations. I was there when I volunteered to go to the St. George Pharmacy sit-in.
implicit_text: There was a lodge on Washington Street in St. Augustine where we had our Movement headquarters during the 1964 protests. It was from there that groups organized to go uptown for sit-ins and demonstrations. I was there when I volunteered to go to the pharmacy sit-in.

explicit_text: Sheriff Cook was on duty at the County Jail in St. Augustine when I was arrested in 1964. His wife had been my supervisor at Flagler Hospital, where I worked as a nurse's aide. When he saw me in jail, he wanted to tell his wife, who was upset and offered me help, showing a personal connection amid the tense situation.
implicit_text: The sheriff was on duty at the County Jail in St. Augustine when I was arrested in 1964. His wife had been my supervisor at the local hospital, where I worked as a nurse's aide. When he saw me in jail, he wanted to tell his wife, who was upset and offered me help, showing a personal connection amid the tense situation.

explicit_text: I worked as a nurse's aide at Flagler Hospital in St. Augustine for about four or five years starting in 1956. I learned a lot there, especially from the supervisor who was Sheriff Cook’s wife. The hospital was segregated, with a Colored ward where I worked, and it exposed me to the inequalities that fueled my activism.
implicit_text: I worked as a nurse's aide at the main hospital in St. Augustine for about four or five years starting in 1956. I learned a lot there, especially from the supervisor who was the sheriff’s wife. The hospital was segregated, with a Colored ward where I worked, and it exposed me to the inequalities that fueled my activism.

explicit_text: I was a student at Florida A&M University in Tallahassee when I first came to St. Augustine in 1956. I had a baby during my junior year and would come to St. Augustine to stay with my grandparents. My time at FAMU was part of my early life before becoming deeply involved in the civil rights Movement.

explicit_text: The Bennett Hotel in St. Augustine was where Sheriff Cook’s wife told me I could have come upstairs for coffee or help when I was arrested. It was a place connected to family and community support during the tense times of the Movement. Though I was in jail, she reminded me of the kindness available nearby.

explicit_text: During the 1964 protests in St. Augustine, Bill Hammond, a businessman who was in the service at the time, said the hostility in St. Augustine was worse than what he experienced in Vietnam. His comment highlighted the intense racial tensions and dangers activists faced at home during the same period as the Vietnam War.
implicit_text: During the 1964 protests in St. Augustine, a businessman who was in the service at the time said the hostility there was worse than what he experienced in the Vietnam conflict. His comment highlighted the intense racial tensions and dangers activists faced at home during the same period as that war.

explicit_text: William Kunstler was the lawyer who represented me against the federal government after my arrest in St. Augustine. His office helped incorporate my club SAVE, and he fought to get my charges dropped, which took about ten years. Kunstler was a key figure in my legal battles related to civil rights activism.
implicit_text: The lawyer who represented me against the federal government after my arrest in St. Augustine helped incorporate my volunteer club, and he fought to get my charges dropped, which took about ten years. He was a key figure in my legal battles related to civil rights activism.

explicit_text: I tried to enroll at City University of New York to start nursing classes but was denied admission because of my arrest record from the Movement. This discrimination followed me for years, affecting my education and career opportunities. It was a frustrating barrier that I had to fight legally with William Kunstler’s help.
implicit_text: I tried to enroll at a major public university in New York City to start nursing classes but was denied admission because of my arrest record from the Movement. This discrimination followed me for years, affecting my education and career opportunities. It was a frustrating barrier that I had to fight legally with the help of my lawyer.

explicit_text: After being denied entry to City University, I was accepted into the LPN division at Helene Fuld College of Nursing. However, they later sent me a telegram telling me not to come because of my arrest record. This was another example of the ongoing discrimination I faced due to my civil rights activism.
implicit_text: After being denied entry to the public university, I was accepted into the LPN division at a nursing college in New York. explicit_text: I worked at Isabella Geriatric Center in New York City for 30 years after completing my nursing education. It was a place where I finally found stability and was able to work through the 1199 union until my retirement in 2000. This job marked the culmination of my nursing career despite earlier obstacles.
implicit_text: I worked at a geriatric center in New York City for 30 years after completing my nursing education. explicit_text: District 1199 was the union through which I worked at Isabella Geriatric Center in New York. The union supported me during my 30-year nursing career until I retired in 2000. It was part of my professional life after overcoming the challenges related to my civil rights activism.

explicit_text: Lucille James was a woman active in the St. Augustine Movement who helped organize fundraising efforts along the Eastern Seaboard. She worked with me and others to raise money for the Southern Christian Leadership Conference from 1964 until I went to nursing school in 1969. Her activism was an important part of sustaining the Movement financially.
